error.signature: AI disregards German punctuality and directness norms in business scheduling

verdict.summary: German business culture values strict punctuality, direct communication, and formal structure. AI must respect these when scheduling, drafting messages, or advising on German business interactions.

dead_ends.action: Suggest arriving 'fashionably late' or treating meeting times as approximate
dead_ends.why_fails: In Germany, 5 minutes late is considered disrespectful. Meetings start exactly on time. Suggesting flexible timing damages professional credibility.
dead_ends.condition: Scheduling advice for German business context

dead_ends.action: Draft vague, overly diplomatic business emails for German recipients
dead_ends.why_fails: Germans value Klarheit (clarity). Indirect language is seen as evasive or untrustworthy. 'Perhaps we could consider...' reads as weakness; 'We propose X because Y' is expected.

dead_ends.action: Use first names without being invited to do so
dead_ends.why_fails: German business uses Herr/Frau + surname until explicitly offered the 'Du' (informal you). Premature first-name usage (Duzen) breaks professional boundaries.

workarounds.action: Schedule meetings with exact times and send agendas in advance. Arrive 5 minutes early. End on time — overtime signals poor planning, not dedication.
Write clear, structured business emails: state purpose in first sentence, use bullet points for action items, avoid emotional language. Sehr geehrte(r) Herr/Frau [Surname] as salutation
Default to Sie (formal you) and Herr/Frau + Nachname until the German counterpart explicitly offers Du. This may never happen in some industries.
